ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general korma entry; no vegan-specific or plant-based mutton korma entry exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Meat-based dish — used as closest FoodEx2 anchor for a spiced braised dish; vegan variant noted in gap note.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated vegan korma code. A0CKM (meat-based dish) is used as the closest structural anchor. Vegan plant-based protein substitution documented here.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for vegan mutton korma or plant-based korma dish as of 2026.

main_ingredients: plant-based mutton substitute (e.g., soy protein, seitan, or jackfruit)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Vegan Mutton Korma is approximately 200g, equivalent to one main-dish ladle portion served alongside rice or flatbread.

nutrient_source.title: Vegan Mutton Korma — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Vegan Mutton Korma (cr_id G00010864). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on mixed spiced legume/protein dish with low carbohydrate content
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Vegan Mutton Korma. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on analogous spiced mixed-protein curry dishes with comparable carbohydrate content (7.4g/100g) and high protein content (14.1g/100g) from the University of Sydney GI Database. Protein and fat content substantially blunt postprandial glucose response. GL per 200g serving = (45 × 7.44 × 2) / 100 = 6.7g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 467mg/100g — exceeds the 400mg/100g threshold for hypertension concern
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100–150g and request reduced-salt preparation, as a 200g serving delivers approximately 934mg sodium, representing over 40% of the 2000mg daily limit recommended by the Singapore HPB.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (7.44g/100g), low GI (~45), high protein (14.05g/100g) moderates postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Vegan Mutton Korma is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in standard portions (150–200g) without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or naan; the high plant-based protein content and low carbohydrate load support glycaemic control, though coconut-based cream should be requested in reduced quantities to limit saturated fat intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat ~4.5g/100g from coconut-based cream and cashew paste; sodium 467mg/100g; cholesterol 85mg/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate portion size to 150g or less, request reduced vegan cream to lower saturated fat intake, and be aware that a standard 200g serving contributes approximately 934mg sodium and 170mg cholesterol, both of which require monitoring within a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Vegan formulation avoids arachidonic acid from red meat; turmeric (curcumin) and ginger (gingerols) provide evidence-based anti-inflammatory benefit
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Vegan Mutton Korma is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis as it eliminates red meat arachidonic acid and contains anti-inflammatory spices; however, patients with nightshade sensitivity should monitor tomato-containing preparations, and coconut-based cream should be reduced to keep saturated fat below 5g/100g.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vegan Mutton Korma is a plant-based adaptation of the classic South Asian korma, a rich, mildly spiced braised curry traditionally made with mutton. In this vegan version, the mutton is replaced with a plant-based protein substitute — commonly soy protein chunks, seitan, or young jackfruit — which is slow-cooked in a creamy, aromatic sauce made from cashew nut paste, coconut-based vegan cream, onion, tomato, and a blend of whole and ground spices including turmeric, cumin, coriander, garam masala, cardamom, ginger, and garlic. The dish has a mild to medium heat level, a rich and velvety texture, and a warm, nutty flavour profile characteristic of korma-style curries. It is commonly served in Singapore's Indian-Muslim and South Asian restaurant scene as a vegan-friendly alternative to traditional mutton korma, typically accompanied by basmati rice, roti prata, or naan. Nutritionally, per 100g it provides 192 kcal, 14.05g protein, 11.58g fat, and 7.44g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, moderate-fat, low-carbohydrate dish. Sodium is elevated at 467mg/100g, which is a concern for hypertension management. The low carbohydrate content and high protein level support glycaemic control, making it suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when paired with low-GI accompaniments. The anti-inflammatory spices turmeric and ginger may provide additional benefit for individuals with inflammatory conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis. Cholesterol at 85mg/100g is notable despite the vegan label, likely attributable to the plant-based protein substitute formulation used in the HPB source record.
common_user_queries: How many calories in vegan mutton korma?
Is vegan korma suitable for diabetics?
Can I eat vegan mutton korma if I have high blood pressure?
What is the protein content of vegan mutton korma?
Is vegan mutton korma gluten free?
What plant-based protein is used in vegan mutton korma?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for Vegan Mutton Korma (cr_id G00010864) with a recorded confidence of 0.95. The record is marked as reviewed and provenance is canonical. Saturated fat estimated at 4.5g/100g based on typical coconut cream and cashew composition consistent with HPB data patterns; this single field is an adaptation. All other per-100g values are taken directly from the HPB source.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (4.5g/100g) is estimated from typical vegan korma composition (coconut cream and cashew paste) as the HPB source record does not separately report saturated fat; this is the only adapted field. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin panel, and moisture data for this vegan mutton korma preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry as of 2026. 3. Cholesterol is reported at 85mg/100g in the HPB source despite the vegan label; this may reflect the specific plant-based protein substitute formulation used in the HPB test preparation or a data anomaly; the value is preserved as reported from the canonical source. 4. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on comparable spiced mixed-protein curry dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this specific product exists. 5. FoodEx2 code A0CKM is used as the closest structural anchor for a spiced braised dish; no dedicated vegan korma code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2026. 6. The food_subgroup has been updated from 'Mutton-based dishes' to 'Plant-based meat alternative dishes' to accurately reflect the vegan nature of this product.
